q: What classic movie would you pull off the shelf for someone trying to heal a broken heart?
a: The Umbrellas of Cherbourg, because it's a pretty epic love story.

q: What is your Mount Rushmore, your top four cured meats of all time?
a: That is the hardest question I have ever been asked, because right now I cannot think of a single one.

q: Do you have an all-time favorite Ed in the Wild fan experience?
a: Bringing Courteney Cox to his tiny village's Tesco, which she thought was a farmer's market, so locals saw Monica from Friends browsing the pasta.

q: What is your favorite guitar in your collection?
a: One Eric Clapton gave me off his wall that survived a studio fire, got restored, and still smells like burnt wood.

q: What is the number one song guaranteed to fill a dance floor?
a: Mony Mony, because it cuts across all age groups.

q: Who is the most powerful Jedi in the Star Wars saga?
a: Yoda, obviously, even though he admits he cannot back the claim up with evidence.

q: What is your heavy metal album Mount Rushmore?
a: Ride the Lightning, Master of Puppets, and ...And Justice for All, basically an all-Metallica Mount Rushmore.

q: What is your favorite show of all time?
a: South Park, for where it met me in my life and the stretch it gave me.

q: What is your Mount Rushmore of cereals?
a: Lucky Charms, Kix, Cocoa Puffs, and Cocoa Rice Krispies.

q: What is your favorite flavor of ice cream?
a: Strawberry, hands down.

q: What's your Mount Rushmore of Nicolas Cage movies?
a: Raising Arizona, Peggy Sue Got Married, Face/Off, and Moonstruck.

q: How would you describe a perfect Sunday, or a proper knees-up?
a: A roast at a pub, day drinking, and then dancing to disco music until the early hours.

q: What are your top three Christmas movies?
a: Christmas Vacation is number one, then It's a Wonderful Life and A Christmas Story.

q: What do you want for the holidays?
a: I am perfectly happy with a candle and some earrings.

q: What is your go-to karaoke song?
a: Any Man of Mine by Shania Twain, and yes I do the whoop.

q: What reality TV are you not ashamed to love?
a: I'm a Bravo gal, and Real Housewives of Salt Lake City is one of my favorites because it's just bonkers.

q: Guns to your head, who is your favorite Backstreet Boy?
a: Kevin, because he's a fox.

q: How do you make your peanut butter and jelly sandwich, the build and the ratio?
a: Smooth peanut butter on both sides of the bread so the grape jam stays contained, a technique tied to fond childhood lunches with his mom.

q: What's a grocery or cooking hack for musicians on a tight budget?
a: Beans on toast.

q: What is your simple grocery hack for a struggling musician on a budget?
a: Beans on toast, one of my favorite English meals.

q: When a restaurant asks how spicy you want your food on a scale of one to ten, what do you say?
a: I always say five.

q: What's been the greatest triumph of your cooking tutorials, and your biggest fail?
a: Her triumph was discovering how easy it is to make your own nut milk, and her fail was a disgusting coffee cake from one of her earliest videos at age 16.

q: What four cheeses have to be on your dream cheeseboard?
a: A goat cheese, a gouda, a crunchy not-too-sharp cheddar, and a fourth she runs out of breath to name.

q: What is your tip for handling spicy food?
a: Breathe out steadily instead of in, because breathing in is just oxygen to the flame.

q: Where does the best Colombian food in the US live?
a: New York, because so many Colombians moved to Queens that some spots taste even more Colombian than back home.

q: What is your relationship with ranch dressing?
a: I love ranch and once had a whole ranch-flavored birthday cake, I wish I had some right now.

q: What makes the Australian burger the best in the world?
a: Australians put everything on them, eggs, bacon, avocado, a patty, six cheeses, and mayonnaise, because the more the merrier.

q: What is your go-to stadium food at a match?
a: Some form of a sausage on bread, because I have a very simple palate.

q: How would you describe your ideal Kansas City barbecue crawl?
a: Burnt ends from Gates, pulled pork from Kansas City Joe's, sides from Jack Stack, turkey from Q39, and brisket from Arthur Bryant's.

q: What's the secret to a perfect guacamole?
a: A whole lot of lime, plus jalapenos, cilantro, and good ripe avocados; she hates when people just toss a little pico de gallo on top.

q: What is a surprising tip for cooking a great steak?
a: Pound the steak mercilessly with a baseball bat before grilling, which is also a little cathartic.

q: Where do you fall on ketchup on a burger?
a: Big yes, so much that when you put the top bun on, it just runs off the sides.

q: What's your drunk midnight grilled cheese recipe?
a: Munster cheese with mushrooms sauteed in butter and teriyaki sauce, plus garlic salt and garlic powder on the buttered bread.

q: What's your favorite drunk sandwich?
a: A tuna sandwich with brie, salami, basil, and honey mustard.

q: What food spot near where you grew up still makes the best pizza you've had?
a: Tony's Fish Bar in West Lothian, which also does a good garlic mayonnaise.

q: How did you prepare yourself for the spice of this show?
a: I had an addiction to Hot Cheetos and survived the One Chip Challenge, so I figured this would be a piece of cake.

q: As an aficionado of the art form, what are the keys to a perfect onion ring?
a: A thickly cut onion with a crisp yet thick battered crust, and crucially the onion must break cleanly on the bite instead of pulling out in threads.

q: What are the keys to a perfect onion ring?
a: A thickly cut onion with a crisp but substantial battered crust, where the onion breaks cleanly on the bite instead of pulling out in threads.

q: How do you feel about cheese?
a: I'm a cheese connoisseur and the zanier the better, I've never met a cheese that was too funky and crazy for me.

q: What is the best way to eat really hard ice cream?
a: Use a fork, because it has less surface area contact until the ice cream gets runny.

q: What is a macad, your favorite on-set snack, and how do you make one?
a: Take a piece of lettuce, add ham and peppers, cover the whole thing in sriracha, and skip the bread entirely.

q: Where outside of Mexico does tacos best?
a: Any one of my houses.

q: Has success changed your love of Costco hot dogs?
a: No, if I'm still eating Top Ramen then you know I'm for sure still eating Costco hot dogs.

q: Which Asian snacks do you think belong in every gas station across the country?
a: Shrimp chips and Hello Panda cookies.

q: What's the difference between the best and worst popcorn in the NBA?
a: The best, at Dallas Mavericks games, nails the presentation, butter-to-salt ratio, and crispiness, while the worst is mushy.

q: You eat burritos sideways and people roast you for it. What's your defense?
a: Be proud of how you eat your burritos, because when you eat one sideways it eventually turns into a taco.

q: What's the weirdest thing about leaving a restaurant?
a: They hover over you the whole meal, then vanish the second you're done, so leaving takes double the time it took to eat.

q: How spicy do you order at the restaurant Nando's?
a: A medium, or a hot if I'm feeling adventurous, because extra hot is too far gone for me.

q: What is your honest opinion on Doritos?
a: I love them, especially on a plane, but now that I am older I do think a little more about what is in them.

q: What is a food you cannot resist?
a: The Portillo's cake shake, it is basically a whole cake blended into a shake and it is incredible.

q: What is your favorite home-cooked meal?
a: My husband can cook a steak better than any restaurant, with a nice rub on it, and it's almost ruined getting a steak out.

q: Who do you call one of the greatest rappers to ever live?
a: William Shakespeare, because studying his verse and meter helped me rap on camera.

q: Are peanut butter, banana, and bacon sandwiches overrated or underrated?
a: Underrated, because people treat them like a joke but they're really delicious.

q: Is New York City actually dead?
a: No, it's just constantly changing, which is something New Yorkers always complain about but it never stops.

q: What is the single worst job in all of media?
a: Red carpet correspondent, where you memorize a phone-book-thick binder just to chase a soundbite as someone walks past.

q: Are you thumbs up or thumbs down on Chicago-style deep dish pizza?
a: Thumbs down; he thinks pizza is overrated, basically just an open grilled-cheese sandwich.

q: Thumbs up or thumbs down on Chicago style deep dish pizza?
a: Thumbs down, because pizza is really just an open sandwich and it's overrated.

q: Why have you decided to stay off social media entirely?
a: I honestly don't think I'm mentally strong enough, and I know I'd end up in a fight with a random person on Twitter.

q: Why can a scary video game be more frightening than even the most suspenseful thriller?
a: Because it is immersive and you are the one choosing to walk down that dark hallway, not just watching.

q: What separates a good peanut butter from a mediocre one?
a: A good one is all natural, just peanuts and salt, because Jif tastes too fake, like icing.

q: What was the biggest fight with your home design team?
a: They hated the idea of a TV above the fireplace, but I told them not to tell me how to live when I watch a lot of college football.

q: What is one of your biggest pet peeves when watching actors eat on camera?
a: When someone just pushes the food around the plate instead of actually eating it.

q: What is your hot take on spicy food once you push through the pain?
a: It is like leg day at the gym, the burn means you are getting stronger, and spicy is the best.

q: Do you have a defense for the Australian burger after David Chang called them an abomination for putting beets on them?
a: He insists Australians make the best burgers because they pile on everything, eggs, bacon, avocado, the patty, six cheeses and mayo, though he draws the line at beets and pineapple.

q: How do you feel about mixing fruit and meat in a dish?
a: For me there's absolutely no chance, my mom's mango chicken salad tasted good but it made me so mad.

q: How do you feel about a guacamole recipe that adds green peas to the avocado?
a: That's not even controversial, that's obviously incorrect.

q: Where do you stand on the strongest, most powerful Jedi in the Star Wars saga?
a: Yoda, obviously, even though I can't back it up with novels or wiki pages.

q: What's an appropriate amount of money to spend on a first date?
a: Nothing, because you might never meet that person again.

q: Is hokey pokey ice cream overrated or underrated?
a: I like it, but I'm more preferential to cookies and cream, which I think of as a very New Zealand ice cream.

q: Is a turkey leg hot girl behavior or not?
a: Hot girl behavior, the turkey just falls right off the bone.

q: What's your response to people who think an oatmeal raisin cookie can never be better than chocolate chip?
a: She hates chocolate chip cookies and defends oatmeal raisin as the best, arguing they're hearty and somewhat healthy because oatmeal has fiber.

q: Is there good Mexican food in New York?
a: No, there's no good Mexican food in New York, come for me.

q: What's the biggest misconception about Magic: The Gathering and the people who play it?
a: That it's nerdy, but he's happily a nerd who'd rather sit in his room playing cards all day than go to a strip club, and it's pretty normal.

q: Why do video game soundtracks deserve more respect?
a: Some of the best, most timeless music comes from games, like Zelda, Skyrim, and the Mario theme that everyone knows.

q: Can you explain your theory that thick patties are for dickheads?
a: I love a thin, crispy burger and would rather eat several small burgers than one big thick-pattied one.

q: What is your everyday gripe about leaving a restaurant?
a: Servers won't stop talking to you while your mouth is full, but the second you're done, nobody comes to bring the check, so leaving takes double the time it took to eat.

q: Say something nice about your rival, the Kansas City Chiefs.
a: The nicest thing I can say is that they had a good run. (Kevin Hart)

q: What is a habit of grown adults at the airport that you cannot get behind?
a: Grown adults riding their suitcases, and a man doing it is a hard no for me.

q: What is your honest take on watching comedy?
a: I don't enjoy comedy, even comedy that's done well, pass.

q: What is your honest hot take on a famous playwright everyone praises?
a: Most people don't see Shakespeare as deep, and at school I would fall dead asleep within five minutes.

q: What scene type would you happily skip?
a: Love scenes, that's the perfect time to get up and go to the bathroom because you won't miss anything.

q: What is something only children miss out on?
a: Conflict, because my friends with siblings are so much better at moving through it quickly instead of amplifying it.

q: What is a hobby that confuses you about your friends?
a: Golf, because every guy I know got obsessed with it overnight and they all seem to hate it and themselves but want to do it all the time.

q: Finish the sentence: 'One thing I'll never apologize for is...'
a: My mouth, though sometimes I admit I am a little too hard and do apologize.

q: Cosmopolitans, guilty pleasure or timeless classic?
a: When made right with fresh ingredients they're very refreshing, and I love a pink cocktail because it reminds me of vacation.

q: Which would you consider the greater cinematic masterpiece, Sharknado or Big Ass Spider?
a: Big Ass Spider for sure.

q: Drumsticks or flats on a chicken wing?
a: Flats, because I love getting in between them, with a good drumstick every now and again.

q: On an alien planet, who would you trust most with your life, Captain Janeway or Jean-Luc Picard?
a: Jean-Luc Picard, because I love him so much I once had a ringtone that said 'Captain Jean-Luc Picard, USS Enterprise.'

q: Oatmeal raisin cookies versus chocolate chip cookies: which side are you on?
a: Oatmeal raisin all the way, because chocolate chip cookies are just not it for me.

q: Which Italian carmaker makes better sports cars, Ferrari or Lamborghini?
a: Lamborghini, hands down.

q: Which Italian manufacturer makes better sports cars, Ferrari or Lamborghini?
a: Lamborghini.

q: Raw oysters or cooked?
a: Raw oysters, all the way.

q: Would you rather eat one big burger or several small ones?
a: Several small ones, because a thin crispy burger beats a thick patty any day.

q: Lakes or ocean?
a: I love both, but lakes are easier because I'm not as scared of what's in the water.

q: Are you more competitive in golf or basketball?
a: Golf makes me more mad, because I'm just an average hack and I want to be good at it.

q: Are you more nervous on the free throw line or on the first tee?
a: The first tee, not even close, I'm shaking so bad I can't even get the ball to stay on the tee.

q: What three networks would you mash together for your dream show?
a: HGTV, ESPN, and Food Network, combining home improvement, sports, and cooking.

q: Who do you think would have made the funniest comedian if they were not a wrestler?
a: The Rock, because he is witty, likable, and quick on his feet with a microphone.

q: What is a totally different job you would love to try someday?
a: Being a kindergarten teacher, so I could infect the youth with freshness.

q: Is there a wild career idea you've had for life after music?
a: I'd love to be a kindergarten teacher for about ten years and infect the youth with that freshness.

q: If aliens visited Earth and you could only greet them with one of your songs, which would you pick?
a: Embrace the Martian.

q: If you were CEO of YouTube for a day, what's the first thing you'd do?
a: Bring back the public dislike counter because it's a useful signal.

q: What animal would you want to be reincarnated as?
a: A jaguar, because they have always been his favorite animal and the coolest in the world.

q: Why do you want to be reincarnated as a jaguar?
a: They have always been my favorite animal and the coolest animals in the world.

q: What animal do you feel a spiritual connection to, and why?
a: Humpback whales, because they float and eat all day, they're loud, and they're queens of the sea.

q: If you walked into a country pub and someone handed you a guitar, what song would you play to win the room over?
a: The Fields of Athenry, a big Irish pub anthem that everyone sings along to.

q: What is the song you'd most want to hear at your funeral?
a: Prince's Purple Rain, because it's the most emotionally cathartic and musically sophisticated song I can think of.

q: Can you give me a ghost-hunting starter pack?
a: An EMF detector, a voice recorder (preferably analog), and a camera with infrared or night-shot, and then you're good to go.

q: What dream business would you love to open someday?
a: A garden centre out in the English countryside.

q: Which singing princess would make the best feature on your album?
a: Tinkerbell, because she's sassy, she's about her business, and she don't take no shit.

q: If you opened an animal sanctuary, what would it look like?
a: A self-sustaining farm where my fiancee and I grow our own food and live surrounded by tons of animals.

q: If you opened a restaurant, what would it be?
a: A barbecue joint called the Rib Crib, because I've had a weird obsession with ribs since I was a kid.

q: What touchdown celebration do you want Travis to do for you?
a: Sit down in the end zone with your legs crossed, pretend you're holding a teacup, and stick your pinky out.

q: If you could swap bodies with anyone, who would it be?
a: An athlete in that one perfect moment in the zone, like Usain Bolt breaking the world record.

q: If you were on a fancy yacht show, what job would you want?
a: I'd take over the laundry, pop in one earbud, listen to a murder podcast, and fold everything into a perfect color-coded system.

q: If you could sing with any musician, who would it be?
a: Joni Mitchell, though I would just have to do a one-note harmony while she danced all over the place.

q: What is the greatest lengths you have gone to in order to master a skill?
a: I spent three months using only my left foot for everything until it felt as natural as my right.

q: What was one of the best days of your life?
a: Golfing with John Daly, who can sneeze and send the ball 350 yards straight down the line.

q: How much ketchup do you put on a burger?
a: A ridiculous amount, so much that it runs off the sides when you put the top bun on.

q: What's the most oysters you've ever eaten in one sitting?
a: Two dozen, one for an appetizer and one to close out dinner.

q: How long does it take you to make a recipe on your cooking show?
a: The whole point of the show is that I do not know how, so even after a few seasons a recipe can take me 5 to 7 hours.

q: How many grandchildren do you have now?
a: Thirteen.

q: What is a hobby you are weirdly intense about?
a: Legos, I built about thirty sets in forty days and I can do just under five hundred pieces an hour.

q: What 1990s family movie had a big impact on you as a kid?
a: Air Bud, the 1997 film about a dog that shoots hoops, along with Homeward Bound.

q: What is the first time you remember falling in love?
a: I was seven or eight watching Marilyn Monroe, and I just wanted to rescue her and make her happy.

q: How did your parents shape your tolerance for spicy food?
a: Since I was a kid they had me try spicy peppers, sweating with the windows open, telling me to keep going, so I trained my taste buds.

q: When did you first realize you liked to scare people?
a: As a kid I never flinched when people jumped out at me, so people said I had no soul and I started scaring them back.

q: As a kid, did you ever sneak somewhere you were not supposed to be?
a: Yes, we snuck through the back door at Universal to ride rides for free until they caught us.

q: How old were you when you started performing in pubs, and how did you get away with it?
a: He started at 11, hiding in the bathroom beforehand because he was underage.

q: Do you remember the first local talent show you ever won?
a: A Boys and Girls Club singing contest where she won a trophy as tall as her and a $100 check, which felt like a fortune at eight years old.

q: How did you react when you won your first local talent show as a kid?
a: They gave me a trophy as tall as me and a hundred dollar check, and at eight years old I felt rich enough to buy an American Girl doll.

q: Who raised you growing up?
a: I didn't go to church, I was raised by HBO, Spielberg, and Prince.

q: What is your biggest pet peeve from working a customer service job?
a: Do not get huffy when I have to look up a product with no barcode, because I am a human and cannot scan it with my eyes.

q: What surprising job were you most proud of landing as a teenager?
a: Becoming an Abercrombie and Fitch model after walking in with a resume because he wanted to be cool so badly.

q: How is your name actually spelled, and where did that spelling come from?
a: My birth certificate says D-I-O-N, but I spell it D-E-I-O-N because I got it from the Old McDonald song's E-I-E-I-O.

q: How did you play football as a kid if your dad was not sporty?
a: I bought a helmet and shoulder pads at a garage sale and threw a Nerf football to myself by a chain-link fence. (Jimmy Fallon)

q: When you first got an agent as a kid, what did they want to change about you?
a: They said my name was too ethnic and renamed me Lenny Williams, until my dad ripped up the photo and said over my dead body. (Leonardo DiCaprio)

q: Before you made it, what was your worst job?
a: Definitely a bank teller, where customers would actually complain about how bad I was and I'd break out in flop sweat.

q: Did you ever get a middle or high school superlative?
a: Most Talkative, two years in a row, and then I talked my way right out of the building.

q: What nicknames did you grow up with?
a: My brothers called me Nitro because I was so hyper, and my friends call me Ken from the Barbie movie.

q: What was the first role you ever played as a little kid?
a: I played Dorothy in the Wizard of Oz at age four, and I was very tornado-forward, mostly just screaming.

q: Did you like the animals on the farm where you grew up?
a: No, my brother and I were both petrified of the horses because they are so scary, and I hated shoveling out the stalls.

q: What did you collect as a kid?
a: I would take home empty clam, mussel, and lobster shells, rinse off the marinara sauce, and get in the bathtub with them.

q: What was one of the best days of your childhood?
a: My friend and I found a fox skull with an earwig inside it in someone's hedges, and we thought the world was made of magic.

q: What kind of kid were you?
a: A bit of a troublemaker after my parents divorced, I got suspended a handful of times and once had police come to my house over prank phone calls.

q: Where did your nickname come from?
a: It was my old MSN screen name, and it basically meant Charli kiss me.

q: Your parents came to the illegal warehouse raves you performed at, didn't they?
a: Yes, and at the time I thought they were cramping my style, but they were just being very supportive.

q: What was your very first job?
a: Repairing rented breast pumps, which meant scrubbing the dried milk off them with a toothbrush and some spray.

q: What did you do as a kid to beat the heat?
a: My mate and I would go sit in the big food freezer in the shed and eat chocolate bars, because it was so hot out in the Outback.

q: What is something you tried and were genuinely terrible at?
a: I did a year of a law degree and failed, like, abysmally.

q: What is the boldest thing you did to chase your dream?
a: At fourteen I made a PowerPoint called Project Hollywood to convince my parents to let me drop out of school and move to LA.

q: What was your first job?
a: I worked at a dog bakery called Three Dog Bakery at the Grove when I moved to LA at 15, for maybe six months.

q: Why did you change your name to Emma?
a: Emily Stone was taken at the Screen Actors Guild, I first picked Riley, then went with Emma because I was a huge Spice Girls fan and crazy about Baby Spice, Emma.

q: What part did you play in your high school musical?
a: I played Judas in our senior year production of Godspell.

q: What was your job right before you got cast in Mad Men?
a: I was unemployed, I tested for seven pilots that season and biffed them all, and Mad Men was my eighth test.

q: As a kid, what did you dream of being when you grew up?
a: A mom, and at 16 I decided I wanted six kids.

q: What was one of your strangest early jobs?
a: I scraped the glaze off the floor of a donut shop for a little money and some free donut holes, and I was the best glaze scraper in San Diego.

q: What was your first job in Los Angeles?
a: LA Fitness, the 5 AM to 9 AM shift, making four bucks an hour, just checking people in and giving them locker keys.

q: How did you handle a big audition in New York City as a kid?
a: At twelve, my brother and I took a Greyhound bus from Maine, found our own hotel, and I washed my underwear in the sink to wear the same outfit for the callback.

q: What is your best advice for surviving spicy wings?
a: Don't breathe out hard, don't lick your lips, and only use one hand.

q: Why is feeding a pet goldfish a good test for a people-pleasing kid?
a: Because you desperately want the fish to like you and feed it again and again, but you have to set a boundary or you will kill it.

q: What is your favorite life lesson from Sesame Street?
a: Oscar the Grouch never gives up on selling his spaghetti-and-meatballs ice cream, even when everyone only wants strawberry.

q: What is the best advice you have ever received?
a: Most of the thousands of crises in your life never actually happen, so do not give false drama any credit.

q: Do you have a hot girl tip for Mortal Kombat beginners?
a: Google a character's cheat codes, pick one move and memorize it, and you'll beat anyone; Mileena was the first character she ever looked up.

q: How do you decide how worried to be about a problem?
a: I rank everything on a scale where zombies are a ten, so most things turn out to be a two or three I can handle.

q: What is the best piece of advice a parent ever gave you?
a: The toes you step on on the way to the top are connected to the rear ends you will have to kiss on the way back down.

q: What is the secret to being a cool dad?
a: Constantly and lovingly make fun of them.

q: What is one styling rule you swear by?
a: Big goes with big, so you can do baggy on the bottom and tight on top, but never the other way or you'll look like a lamp.

q: What is a piece of advice you got that you still think about?
a: Will Forte told me to never write something because you think someone else will find it funny, only write what you think is funny.

q: What is your theory for staying balanced and happy?
a: I have three cups: alone time, one-on-one time, and social time, and all three need to be somewhat full.

q: What is a piece of advice you live by about being on time?
a: My papa used to say if you're on time, you're already late, so I show up 30 minutes early.

q: What is your motto when you are creating something?
a: You have to dare to suck, because sometimes being stupid is when the most genius idea comes out.

q: What surprised you most once you were inside your industry?
a: Just how no one really knows what they are doing.

q: How do you handle being shy but performing in front of people?
a: I'm quite a shy person, I hate getting up on a stage as myself, but I love getting up on a stage as someone else.

q: How do you feel about your job right now?
a: Technically I'm retired, then if I find something I like I unretire, do it, and immediately retire again, so every gig is my last.

q: Best advice you've ever received?
a: Do it scared.

q: What word did Americans use to describe you that you had to look up?
a: Refreshing, which confused me so much that I kept smelling myself to check if my perfume was fruity.

q: What is the weirdest thing you have ever pulled out of the water while fishing?
a: A five foot alligator garfish so big I had to tie the line to a truck and pull it out.

q: What is your prized catch as an avid fisherman?
a: A garfish about five feet long that I pulled out by tying the line to a truck bumper on a movie set.

q: What is the most ridiculous acting exercise you've ever had to do?
a: Pretending to be a chicken and clucking around the room.

q: What's a surprising fact about mushrooms?
a: Mushrooms made the first internet, responding to a tree falling miles away and traveling to decompose it.

q: What do you search for when someone hands you the laptop at a dinner party?
a: Honestly something like a video on how to turn on your printer, or a bunch of Vine videos.

q: What's the backstory on how you ended up in the famous Kardashian Met Gala bathroom selfie?
a: I only went in to wash my hands while holding my breath to avoid the smoke, got squished into the shot, and couldn't escape.

q: What does it mean when someone from the UK says 'rah'?
a: It's a mini wow, like 'rah, that happened.'

q: What's your favorite British phrase for getting drunk?
a: Waved, like 'I got waved, man,' as in ocean, it was rocky.

q: Where did your trick of wearing the same jacket in public for months come from?
a: During a play the paparazzi waited at the same door nightly, so wearing identical clothes made every photo look like the same day and killed their value.

q: What is your favorite fun fact about penguins?
a: Penguins are necessary and I love them, but up close on the ice they are absolutely disgusting.

q: How many music industry people does it take to change a light bulb?
a: One, and 99 to ask each other what they think of it.

q: What's the most bizarre acting exercise you've ever done in a conservatory program?
a: A vocal technique where everyone holds yoga poses until their body vibrates and then makes orgasm sounds.

q: What is the strangest place you have ever performed?
a: Inside an empty swimming pool, because the owner said the acoustics would make it sound like a big show.

q: Has one of your reactions on this show ever become a meme or a gif?
a: He once nearly choked when a piece of chicken lodged in his windpipe across from Jon Bernthal, and briefly feared that would be his legacy.

q: What did you look like the one time you got bald and blue auditioning for Blue Man Group?
a: I looked like a blue peanut M&M, just eyes and tiny teeth with no cheekbones to hang the paint on.

q: How would you describe pulling nine Gs in an F-16?
a: Challenging and painful, like 13 elephants sitting on you while you work very hard not to pass out.

q: What is your secret talent?
a: Ordering at a restaurant, where she covers every base with appetizers, sides, and entrees from each column.

q: What is the coolest thing you have found on YouTube?
a: A channel of elaborate stop-motion footage of fruit and food going rotten, which he found for his curious kids.

q: What's a hidden talent of yours from being a child actor?
a: I can look at a speech once or twice and repeat it back pretty quickly, almost like a parlor trick.

q: What is your trick for handling really spicy food?
a: Breathe backwards and open a window.

q: What is the coolest thing you've ever done?
a: A midnight whale-tracking helicopter expedition over the ice shelf in Antarctica, flying with the doors open looking for orca.

q: What is the trick to flipping a coin so it always lands on the side you want?
a: Give it an off-axis spin so the same face stays up, making an hourglass shape in the air.

q: How did Francis Ford Coppola get you to act uncomfortable in a scene?
a: He put rocks in my shoes and told me to walk normally into the room.

q: What's a whale quality you see most in yourself?
a: Humpback whales; she says they float and eat all day and like to be girls, which she relates to.

q: Is there a bit of slang from home you can never shake no matter how much you travel?
a: Go way out of that, which he says whenever something shocks him.

q: Do you believe in ghosts or have any ghost stories?
a: I don't mess with ghosts and they don't mess with me, but my mom once saw a man at the top of the stairs in a house where someone had died in the basement.

q: What's the story about outwitting the paparazzi that you're most proud of?
a: She dressed a friend in a blonde wig and her tracksuit with a chihuahua to bolt from her house as a decoy, a trick she's pulled about 50 times.

q: What is the story about outwitting the paparazzi that you're most proud of?
a: I'd put a blonde wig and my tracksuit on a friend with one of my chihuahuas, send them running out of my house, and watch all the paparazzi follow the decoy.

q: What does your made-up word 'sliving' mean?
a: It's a combination of slaying and living your best life.

q: Have you had any close encounters with wildlife since moving to your secluded mountain compound?
a: A crafty mama cougar keeps getting through his big fence, and he swears his infrared cameras also caught a glowing orb he thinks was an alien or a ghost.

q: What is a starter pack of equipment someone needs to go ghost hunting?
a: An EMF detector, a voice recorder, and a camera with infrared or night vision.

q: What slang phrase do you want to bring back?
a: FML, which he says nobody has used since around 2015.

q: Give me a pro and a con of having an owl as a pet.
a: On a hot night you can feel the air as it flies by, but the con is they have to eat rats and sometimes she lands on my head with one.

q: Have you ever had a wild encounter with a sea creature while diving?
a: An octopus once explored me with its tentacles while I stayed very calm and still, and then it just left.

q: What surprising organizations did you discover used to make their own branded ashtrays?
a: Disney, NASA, the Department of Defense, and even the FBI all made branded ashtrays back in the day.

q: What is the strangest place you have ever seen a stock photo of yourself pop up in real life?
a: A photo of him doing Zumba was on the wall of a martial arts dojo, and he was also on the cover of an accounting textbook.

q: What is the strangest place you've ever spotted your old stock photos?
a: A photo of him doing Zumba hanging on the wall of a martial arts dojo, and the cover of an accounting textbook.

q: What is hitting a hole in one like for you?
a: I basically blacked out and sprinted 150 yards down the hole, because unlike a long basketball shot, I never expect it to go in.

q: Why do you eat a burrito sideways?
a: Because when you eat a burrito sideways, it eventually turns into a taco.

q: How did you get to experience Comic-Con anthropologically?
a: I borrowed a Stormtrooper uniform and walked the floor in disguise, which was so fun.

q: What does it mean if a dish is minging?
a: It means it's disgusting, like that last wing was minging.

q: What is a British slang word for disgusting that Americans should know?
a: Minging, as in that last wing was minging.

q: Is it a myth that at the NFL Combine you ran the 40 and just kept running out of the building?
a: That's not true, but I did skip the bench press because nobody was going to lay across my hands and bench me in the face.

q: Of all your movie co-stars, who would make the best tag team partner?
a: Easy, Kevin Hart, his wrestling nickname would be Honky Pete.

q: What did it feel like to hear kids quoting The Rock?
a: There was nothing like it, because if they're saying it on the playground, you know they're getting called to the principal's office.

q: Where did you learn everything you know about football?
a: Everything I learned about football, I know from John Madden football.

q: Why did you turn down a karaoke duet with Justin Timberlake?
a: I was too hammered and couldn't even see the words from the stage, so I told him I'd get him next year.

q: How did you react to the 2015 tweet saying you were 30 and wouldn't last much longer?
a: I laughed at it so hard every single time, especially when the same guy said it again the day I turned 40.

q: If two guys who look like us walked up to you, what movie line would we quote?
a: You're my boy, Blue, or maybe milk was a bad choice.

q: How do you react when something scary happens?
a: I go dead calm and start talking really slowly, like my dad's fainting goats but the opposite.

q: What is a hobby you are secretly really good at?
a: Geography; I love memorizing countries and their capitals just for fun.

q: What is an oddly satisfying thing you love watching online?
a: A guy from Drain Cleaning Australia who unclogs disgusting drains and cheers Oh, you little ripper.

q: What is a hobby that surprises people?
a: I love knowing all the countries and their capitals, it is genuinely a hobby of mine.

q: What is a weird thing you do because you feel bad for objects?
a: I rotate my stuffed animals so none of them are stuck in the dark too long, and I picked the lonely leftover pill so it wouldn't be by itself.

q: Are you good at telling stories at a dinner table?
a: I am really bad at it, getting asked to tell a story while everyone looks at me is my nightmare.

q: What is something most people find easy that scares you?
a: Harmonizing genuinely terrifies me, and I am not good at it.

q: What is something fake in your house that people keep mistaking for the real thing?
a: Fake orange peels that look so real people keep trying to throw them away as trash.

q: How did you ask out the person who became your spouse?
a: I sat at the bar drinking Diet Cokes for weeks, then finally just blurted out do you want to go out, which was super uncool.

q: What is the most embarrassing thing you have done as a famous person?
a: I once waved and yelled hey everybody at a tour bus in Beverly Hills, nobody recognized me, and I shrank into a little ball.

q: What kind of kid do you secretly love?
a: A bad kid, like the little one at my wedding who stuck his whole finger in my cake.

q: What is the wildest thing you have done to seem cool to your kid?
a: I jumped out of a plane in Oahu at age 56, and I cussed the entire way down even though it ended up being a terrific experience.

q: What is something you genuinely cannot do?
a: I cannot bake at all.

q: What is the most starstruck you have ever been meeting someone?
a: Working with Meryl Streep I followed her everywhere and the only thing I could think to say to her was you have beautiful skin.

q: What is a weird habit you have with your phone?
a: I have fourteen alarms saved on it and basically never actually turn any of them on.

q: Tell us about meeting someone you idolized.
a: I met Brad Pitt, went for the handshake, switched to a hug, and it almost turned into a kiss, all in slow motion.

q: What is the most embarrassing way you have ever hurt yourself?
a: I got so excited watching a magician's card trick that I ran, slipped in heeled boots, and broke my shoulder the night before meeting the Spice Girls.

q: What is a surprising thing you do not own?
a: A computer, I'm basically a Neo-Luddite now and do everything by typing with my thumbs on a cracked phone.

q: How did it feel to get cast in Top Gun: Maverick?
a: It was a pinch me moment, and if I could tell my 15-year-old self, I would punch him in the face.

q: Do you have a hidden talent people would not expect?
a: I'm a great gift wrapper, and I have a whole gift wrap room with rolls of paper and drawers of tissue.

q: What is the best Halloween costume you have ever worn?
a: My son asked me to be a toilet, so I was a toilet, and when your kid asks that you have to be the best toilet you can be.

q: How big is your family?
a: I'm one of seven kids, my mother did not have a family, she had a litter of Culkins.

q: What is your go-to little break when you only have a few minutes for yourself?
a: The New York Times games, and I love when they tell me I'm a genius, even if I cheat.

q: What is the wildest rule someone ever gave you?
a: A guy who rented me a car off the books said one thing, do not open the trunk ever, and to this day I never found out what was in it.

q: What happened when you played on a co-ed ice hockey team in America?
a: In all the gear I just looked like a small guy, so they'd check me hard, and then I'd take my helmet off and they'd say, oh, sorry, I didn't know you were a girl.

q: First thing you do in the morning and last thing at night?
a: Morning: get his cats off the bed. Night: move his feet so the cats won't scratch them.

q: What is one thing you can't leave the house without?
a: Chapstick, I have a whole bowl of pomegranate Burt's Bees by my sink and open a new one every day.
